data.title: BayCom trades lower as Brean Capital downgrades post abrupt management change
data.description:
BayCom (BCML) was trading lower as Brean Capital cut its recommendation on the stock after the regional bank announced an abrupt management change.

Shares were 12.43% down to $28.61 during Friday afternoon trading.

The holding company for United Business Bank on Thursday said its board approved a leadership transition and appointed three new executives. Christopher Baron was appointed CEO, Kevin Thompson CFO, and William Black executive vice chairman.

President and CEO George Guarini, Sr. Executive Vice President and COO Janet King, and Sr. Executive Vice President and CFO Keary Colwell have each tendered their resignations from the board.

"All three were founding executives of the bank," noted analyst Timothy Coffey.

"The abrupt management change reflects a change in strategy that likely results in a near-term re-rating of the stock," said the analyst in the research note.

"The stock had traded on the premise the company was positioned to sell, while the new management team is focused on organic growth. We believe a valuation premium for a potential sale could come out of the stock given the strategy shift," added the research note.

The stock was downgraded to Neutral from Buy, with the price target lowered to $32.00 from $34.00.

Brean Capital's rating differs from the Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha's Quant rating of Buy.

data.title: Big banks, S&P Global tie up for first credit-default swaps index linked to private credit - report
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and other large Wall Street banks are working with S&P Global (SPGI) to launch an index of credit-default swaps, The Wall Street Journal reported on Friday.

Banks, including Bank of America (BAC), Barclays (BCS) (BCLYF), Deutsche Bank (DB), and Goldman Sachs (GS), are set to begin selling the derivatives next week, according to people familiar with the matter.

The CDX Financials index is expected to protect buyers against defaults by companies included in the index.

"This will be the first credit-default swap product linked to private credit," Nicholas Godec, head of fixed-income tradeables & commodities at S&P Dow Jones Indices, reportedly said.

Private credit funds managed by Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares Management (ARES), and Blackstone (BX) will reportedly make up 12% of the index. Insurers including MetLife (MET) and Radian Group (RDN); banks such as Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) and Truist Financial (TFC); and credit card companies like American Express (AXP) and Capital One Financial (COF) are also set to be a part of the index.

The so-called FINDX index, which rises when the market sentiment on such firms turns negative, is expected to give debt investors and traders a fast way to hedge or short the industry.

Hedge funds, said to be keen for a way to make bets on a downturn in private credit, began trying to short individual stocks and bonds issued by firms that invest in private credit. The process was cumbersome and costly, the report noted, citing one hedge-fund manager.

Investors are said to have sought to get back more than $20B of their investments from private credit firms in Q1 2026, a sign of the increasing concern after a boom in the asset class.

The sector, including Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares (ARES), Blackstone (BX), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and KKR (KKR), received $20.8B in redemption requests during the quarter.

Activist investor Boaz Weinstein's Saba Capital is among investors advocating for the creation of the new product, according to people familiar with the matter.

The hedge fund manager had offered to buy the shares of Blue Owl Capital's (OWL) private credit fund, Blue Owl Capital Corporation II, in March at a 34.9% discount to the dividend reinvestment plan issuance value per share.

Credit-default swaps were long out of favor for their role in the 2008 financial crisis, but are experiencing a renaissance. Most of their activity is in indexes, and index trading hit a record $38 trillion in 2025, according to S&P Global.

data.title: Nike runs through another chief innovation officer
data.description:
Nike (NKE) chief innovation officer Tom Bignall is leaving the company after being in the position for less than one year.

Bignall, whom CEO Elliott Hill elevated to the chief innovation officer last June, is reportedly leaving to pursue other creative interests and philanthropic pursuits. He will be succeeded by Andy Caine, Nike vice president and creative director for sportswear, effective Sunday. Caine will report to Phil McCartney, whom Hill appointed to the newly created position of chief innovation, design, and product officer last year. Notably, Caine will be the fourth person to head innovation at Nike over the last three years.

Shares of Nike (NKE) fell 3.3% in Friday afternoon action and are down more than 33% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: CoreWeave related ETFs in focus as company rises 12% after Anthropic deal
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) is back in the spotlight after announcing a multi-year agreement with Anthropic to lease data center capacity to support the AI lab's growing compute demands for its Claude models, sending CRWV shares up over 12%.

Against this backdrop, the following list examines CoreWeave-related ETFs across AI, technology, cybersecurity, and IPO themes, ranked by their one-month price performance, offering a window into how the broader market is pricing in the CoreWeave growth story beyond the stock itself.

The list is topped by iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY), with a one-month performance of 3.84% and a Quant Rating of Buy (3.87). Invesco NASDAQ Next Gen 100 ETF (QQQJ) follows with a 2.38% gain and a Buy rating (3.52), while Renaissance IPO ETF (IPO) rounds out the positive performers with a modest 0.35% increase, though it carries a Strong Sell rating (1.41).

The lower end of the list includes two cybersecurity-focused ETFs that have experienced negative performance over the past month. Evolve Cyber Security Index ETF Unhedged USD (CYBR.U:CA) declined 1.09%, while Themes Cybersecurity ETF (SPAM) fell 1.36%. Both of these funds are currently not covered by the Quant Rating system.

Here is the list:

  1. iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY), 1 month performance percentage: 3.84%

  2. Invesco NASDAQ Next Gen 100 ETF (QQQJ), 1 month performance percentage: 2.38%

  3. Renaissance IPO ETF (IPO), 1 month performance percentage: 0.35%

  4. Evolve Cyber Security Index ETF Unhedged USD (CYBR.U:CA), 1 month performance percentage: -1.09%

  5. Themes Cybersecurity ETF (SPAM), 1 month performance percentage: -1.36%

data.title: Forward earnings revisions expectations drop for the first time since January
data.description:
Analyst revisions to forward earnings expectations have dropped for the first time since early January, according to Gina Martin Adams, chief market strategist for HB Wealth.

“It is not unusual for revision momentum to turn south just prior to earnings seasons, and in fact estimates dropped in advance of the last three earnings seasons as well,” Adams said on X.

 

 

 

The downward trend has affected the majority of market sectors, with revision momentum turning negative for seven of the eleven sectors, according to the strategist.

Consumer discretionary (XLY) revisions were the worst performers, while energy (XLE) revisions remained the most positive among sectors, she noted.

Current earnings growth projections show analysts are forecasting 12.8% year-over-year EPS growth for S&P 500 (SP500) companies in the first quarter of 2026, slightly below the 12.9% recorded in the fourth quarter of 2025, Adams explained.

Last year, the index recorded 13.4% growth, she added.

“Negative adjustments to expectations may just be getting started given the current contrast between very elevated forecasts and souring economic data amid rising commodity costs,” the chief market strategist said.

Adams warned that the disparity between analyst optimism and deteriorating economic conditions could lead to further revisions downward.

Oil price (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) impacts are likely to begin showing up in negative earnings revisions this month, according to Adams.

“History suggests elevated oil prices have a lagged impact on fundamental conditions, and as long as oil prices remain somewhat elevated, downward revisions may remain a drag on stocks,” she said.

data.title: Michael Burry buys JD, Alibaba shares; adds to Nvidia puts
data.description:
US-listed shares of JD.com (JD) rose over 2% on Friday after famed investor Michael Burry said he bought shares in the Chinese e-commerce giant.

Burry, known for his prescient bets ahead of the subprime mortgage crisis depicted in The Big Short, also bought shares of Alibaba (BABA), he wrote in a Substack post.

“I bought shares in JD.com and Alibaba today. JD is a significant add, and Alibaba is a new position, a little over 6%. JD is a bit more than that,” Burry wrote in the post, adding that the recent weakness in the shares provided “an attractive entry point.”

Shares of Alibaba were marginally down at $127.60 in afternoon trading.

Additionally, Burry said he bought GameStop (GME), adding to his “already decent sized position.” He also bought shares in payment technology company Fiserv (FISV), noting that he believes in the “new leadership.”

Separately, Burry wrote that he added to his Nvidia (NVDA) puts, “this time buying the January 27 Strike 115 puts at 3.30.”

“The other way I think about it is that I am short at about 3% of notional value, and borrowing costs could easily get that high or higher if NVDA really starts falling. I also own NVDA January 27 Strike 100 Puts from earlier, and I continue to hold those,” the post read.

The investor, in February, ignited a fresh debate over the sustainability of the artificial-intelligence boom, questioning whether tech giants’ massive data center spending can continue without damaging balance sheets and earnings.

data.title: Heineken sells brewery stake in Congo as part of broad asset-light strategy
data.description:
Heineken N.V. (HEINY) has sold its shareholding in Bralima, the beer giant's long‑standing Democratic Republic of Congo brewing subsidiary, to Mauritius‑based ELNA Holdings. Going forward, Heineken (HEINY) will now participate in the market mainly through long‑term brand‑licensing agreements rather than direct ownership.

The transaction shifts Bralima's three breweries, distribution network, and workforce to ELNA, while Heineken (HEINY) retains the rights to brands such as Heineken, Primus, Turbo King, Legend, and Mützig and will earn royalties under an asset‑light model aligned with its EverGreen 2030 strategy. ELNA, described as having industrial and logistics experience across Africa and in the DRC, is expected to ensure continuity of operations, local employment, and the availability of Heineken’s (HEINY) brands under locally anchored ownership.

Bralima, formally known as Brasseries, Limonaderies et Malteries, was founded in 1923 in the then Belgian Congo and became majority‑owned by Heineken (HEINY) in 1986, making it one of the group’s longest‑held African assets. Over decades, Bralima grew into a dominant national brewer, at times capturing more than 70% of the Congolese beer market and becoming deeply embedded in the country’s economic and social life, even as it operated through periods of dictatorship, state collapse, and protracted conflict.

The exit from direct ownership of Bralima therefore reflects Heineken's (HEINY) global shift toward a lighter balance sheet structure.

data.title: Italy’s Meloni renews Eni CEO Descalzi, Enel's Cattaneo; boots Leonardo's Cingolani
data.description:
Italian Prime Minister Meloni said Thursday she will dismiss Leonardo (FINMF) (FINMY) CEO Roberto Cingolani while retaining leadership at energy companies Eni (E) and Enel (ENLAY).

Cingolani will be replaced by Lorenzo Mariani, a company veteran and a senior executive at missile-maker MBDA Missile Systems Services, while Eni (E) CEO Claudio Descalzi and Enel's (ENLAY) Flavio Cattaneo will be renewed for another three years.

Descalzi's fifth term as Eni's (E) CEO will start in May after his nomination is ​approved at the company's shareholder meeting, where the Italian government calls the ⁠shots.

"Continuity in Eni's management represents a positive factor given ​the complex geopolitical context and the strong track record," Italian broker Equita said, citing Descalzi's achievements including ‌the ⁠strengthening of the company's upstream business, a pragmatic approach to decarbonization, and setting up new businesses.

The leadership shakeup at Leonardo (FINMF) (FINMY) comes as momentum for change appears to have increased following a failed referendum that weakened Meloni's political standing.

Leonardo (FINMF) (FINMY) shares, which already had declined over reports of the pending CEO change, fell more than 5% in Milan on Friday.

data.title: War alone doesn't explain record-low consumer sentiment - Jim Bianco
data.description:
Consumer sentiment has plunged to its lowest level in the 74-year history of the University of Michigan survey, but at least one prominent analyst believes the Iran war may not be the primary culprit.

The preliminary April reading on Friday fell to 47.6, with survey director Joanne Hsu attributing the decline to the Middle East conflict. She noted that 98% of interviews were completed before the April 7 temporary Iran ceasefire announcement.

Jim Bianco of Bianco Research isn’t buying that explanation entirely. He called Hsu’s reasoning “wanting,” pointing to a disconnect between the record-low confidence reading and financial markets that have experienced only a modest pullback rather than outright panic. 

"Since 1952, we've had our share of wars and 9/11," he wrote in a post on X. "Yet the [current Middle East] conflict has produced a 74-year low among consumers, but only a 3% correction in the stock market (SPY) (VOO). Does this make sense?" Is the Iran war "worse than the Korean War, the historic protests over the Vietnam War in the 1970s, or the never-ending quagmire in Iraq? This was the conflict that drove us to a 74-year low?"

Bianco offers an alternative interpretation: the real driver is the affordability squeeze that has been weighing on households for years. In his view, the war may have served as a catalyst, but the underlying problem is the cumulative rise in prices over the past five or six years.

data.title: U.S. senators call for probe on unusual trading patterns in oil futures
data.description:
U.S. senators Elizabeth Warren and Sheldon Whitehouse are pushing for an investigation into unusual trading patterns seen in oil futures (CL1:COM), noticed immediately preceding two major government announcements related to the Iran conflict.

Warren has sent a letter to Michael Selig, chairman of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission.

"On the morning of March 23, oil futures trading surged dramatically in the minutes before President Trump posted on Truth Social announcing talks with Iran to potentially de-escalate the war—a post that sharply increased stock market indexes and lowered crude prices. There was no public news preceding the announcement to explain the price movement," wrote the Senators.

"The same pattern appears to have recurred on April 7, 2026. In the hours before President Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran—an announcement that sent oil prices down ~15%—traders placed an ~$950M bet on oil prices falling," said the Senators.

The senators raised concerns about recurring misappropriation of material nonpublic government information, and the extent to which individuals inside or outside the government have acted on such information.

Warren and Whitehouse have requested written responses by April 30.
